criteria.label: Explain the BST invariant and why in-order traversal sorts the keys.
criteria.example: Invariant: every key in the left subtree < node.key < every key in the right subtree. In-order visits *left → node → right* — which is exactly the sorted order. That's why an in-order walk emits keys in ascending order without an explicit sort.

criteria.label: Walk a tree pre-order, in-order, post-order and level-order, recursively and iteratively.
criteria.example:
```python
def inorder(n):           # left, self, right
    if not n: return
    yield from inorder(n.left)
    yield n.val
    yield from inorder(n.right)

def levelorder(root):     # BFS
    q = deque([root])
    while q:
        n = q.popleft(); yield n.val
        if n.left: q.append(n.left)
        if n.right: q.append(n.right)
```

criteria.label: Recall the difference between balanced and unbalanced complexity.
criteria.example: Inserting `1, 2, 3, 4, 5` into a plain BST produces a degenerate right chain — search is now O(n). A balanced BST (AVL / red-black) keeps height O(log n) by rotating; same input, search stays O(log n).

criteria.label: Use sortedcontainers.SortedDict when you need an ordered map in Python.
criteria.example: Need 'all sessions with last_seen between t1 and t2'? `from sortedcontainers import SortedDict; sd[t1:t2]` slices in O(log n + k). Implementing a balanced BST by hand for this is a week of work; `SortedDict` is one import.

criteria.label: Implement BST insert and search; understand the three delete cases.
criteria.example: Delete cases: (1) leaf — drop it; (2) one child — splice the child in; (3) two children — replace with in-order successor (leftmost node of the right subtree), then delete the successor (which has at most one child).

criteria.label: Distinguish trees, heaps, and general graphs by invariant and use-case.
criteria.example: Tree: no cycles, one path between any two nodes. Heap: tree with a parent/child ordering invariant (priority queue). Graph: nodes + edges with no shape constraints (social network, road map). Different invariants → different operations cheap → different problems they suit.
